mcqs.question: What is the fundamental difference between a materialized view and a regular (virtual) view in a DBMS?

options.text: Materialized views store data physically while regular views execute the query each time they are accessed
Materialized views can only contain SELECT statements without aggregate functions
Regular views always provide better performance than materialized views
Materialized views cannot be updated automatically

mcqs.explanation: Materialized views store the actual result of the query on disk, acting like a snapshot, while regular views execute the underlying query each time they are accessed.

mcqs.question: Consider a view defined as: CREATE VIEW sales_summary AS SELECT region, SUM(amount) as total_sales FROM sales GROUP BY region; What happens when you execute UPDATE sales_summary SET total_sales = 5000 WHERE region = 'North'?

options.text: The update succeeds and modifies the base table sales
The update fails because aggregate views are not updatable
The update creates a new row in the sales table
The update automatically creates a trigger to modify sales

mcqs.explanation: Views containing GROUP BY, aggregate functions, or DISTINCT are inherently non-updatable because there's no one-to-one mapping between rows in the view and the base table.

mcqs.question: In Oracle DBMS, which refresh method for a materialized view ensures data consistency with the base table at the time of commit?

options.text: REFRESH FORCE
REFRESH COMPLETE
REFRESH ON COMMIT
REFRESH FAST

mcqs.explanation: REFRESH ON COMMIT causes the materialized view to be automatically refreshed whenever a transaction commits changes to the base table, ensuring strong consistency.

mcqs.question: A data warehouse frequently runs queries calculating monthly sales totals. Which optimization technique would provide the BEST performance?

options.text: Creating a regular view with GROUP BY on the fact table
Creating a materialized view with monthly aggregation and enabling query rewrite
Creating an index on the date column of the fact table
Using a stored procedure to compute totals on-the-fly

mcqs.explanation: A materialized view pre-computes and stores aggregated results; query rewrite allows the optimizer to automatically redirect queries to use the materialized view, eliminating redundant computation.

mcqs.question: What is the main disadvantage of using FAST refresh for materialized views?

options.text: It requires more disk space than complete refresh
It requires a materialized view log on the base table and may fail if logs are insufficient
It cannot be used with aggregate functions
It always takes longer than complete refresh

mcqs.explanation: FAST refresh uses a materialized view log to track changes to base tables; without proper logging infrastructure, fast refresh is not possible and the refresh will fail.

mcqs.question: In PostgreSQL, which feature provides functionality similar to materialized views with automatic refresh capabilities?

options.text: Auto-views
Refreshable materialized views using REFRESH CONCURRENTLY
Eager materialized views
System-managed views

mcqs.explanation: PostgreSQL supports REFRESH CONCURRENTLY which allows refreshing materialized views without locking reads, though a unique index is required for this feature.

mcqs.question: Given base tables orders(order_id, customer_id, order_date, amount) and order_items(order_id, product_id, quantity, unit_price), a materialized view mv_customer_sales is created to aggregate total sales per customer. If a new order with 5 line items is inserted, which sequence correctly describes the refresh impact?

options.text: Fast refresh updates only the affected customer row; complete refresh recomputes all rows
Both fast and complete refresh update only the specific customer row in the MV
Fast refresh requires re-computation because multiple base tables are involved
Complete refresh is the only option for joins involving more than one base table

mcqs.explanation: Fast refresh updates only the changed aggregate (the customer whose order was inserted), while complete refresh recomputes all customer aggregates from scratch, making it slower but more reliable.

mcqs.question: A materialized view mv_monthly_revenue is defined with REFRESH FAST ON DEMAND. The application requires near-real-time revenue figures but experiences performance degradation during refreshes. Which strategy would BEST address this conflict in an Oracle environment?

options.text: Change to REFRESH COMPLETE ON COMMIT for real-time updates
Implement refresh on a schedule using DBMS_SCHEDULER during off-peak hours and use stale tolerance
Drop the materialized view and use regular views with indexes
Use REFRESH FORCE with minimal logging

mcqs.explanation: Scheduling refreshes during off-peak hours balances performance with freshness; stale tolerance allows queries to use the MV even if slightly outdated, reducing refresh frequency while maintaining acceptable data currency.
